name: The Mirror: Repeat to Unlock
description: Repeat the last 1-3 words (or a critical phrase) of what the counterparty just said, with upward inflection. Triggers them to elaborate, exposes constraints, and buys you thinking time without disrupting flow.
disclaimer: Mirroring overused sounds parrot-like and condescending. 3-4 mirrors per 30-minute call is normal; 10+ is a tic. Vary with labels and calibrated questions.

context.when_to_use: When you want the counterparty to keep talking — to surface constraints, expand on policy, justify a number, or reveal a hidden lever. Especially useful in the first 10 minutes when you're building a map of what's negotiable.
preconditions: Voice channel (mirrors via email work but are much weaker — they read as snark).
Counterparty has just said something containing a number, policy, jargon term, or hesitation marker.
You can deliver the mirror with curious upward inflection — same words but as a question.

principles: A mirror is the last 1 to 3 words of what they said, repeated as a question. Not paraphrased. Verbatim. The verbatim repetition is what triggers the elaboration instinct.
Pair every mirror with silence (4-6 seconds). The mirror is the question; the silence is the rest of the question.
Pick the words that carry the constraint. If they say 'we can't go below $200 for new accounts', mirror 'new accounts?' — that's where the flex lives.
Mirroring buys you time. While they elaborate, you're free to think about what to ask next or to consult your notes.
Mirrors are non-confrontational. You're not arguing; you're inviting more detail. The counterparty rarely notices the move.
When in doubt between a label and a mirror: use a label for emotion, a mirror for information.

phrases.jargon_mirror: [Rep: 'That account is on a legacy tier.'] → 'A legacy tier?'
phrases.hesitation_mirror: [Rep: 'I don't think I can…'] → 'You don't think you can?'
phrases.mirror_followup_silence: [Just stay quiet for 4-6 seconds — do not soften the mirror.]

tactics.name: Mirror the constraint word
tactics.when: Rep states a rule, policy, or limit with a qualifying word (only, usually, typically, currently, for now).
tactics.script: Repeat the qualifying word back as a question. 'Usually?' / 'For now?' / 'Currently?' — qualifiers signal the rule has exceptions; the mirror invites the exception out.

tactics.name: Mirror the jargon
tactics.when: Rep uses internal terminology ('legacy account', 'grandfathered rate', 'override code', 'CC promo').
tactics.script: Mirror the jargon as a question. The rep will explain — and the explanation will tell you about adjacent tiers and override paths you didn't know existed.

tactics.name: Mirror the number
tactics.when: Rep delivers a price, fee, or limit number.
tactics.script: Mirror the number with upward inflection. Forces the rep to either justify it or move it.

tactics.name: Mirror the hesitation
tactics.when: Rep uses softening verbs: 'I think', 'I believe', 'I might be able to', 'probably'.
tactics.script: Mirror the hedge. 'You think?' / 'Might be able to?' — converts a soft maybe into either a firm yes or a firm no, which is more actionable than soft maybe.

tactics.name: Mirror to stall
tactics.when: You need 5-10 seconds to think and don't want a verbal 'um' or 'let me think'.
tactics.script: Mirror the last few words of what the rep said. The rep will spend 20-30 seconds elaborating, giving you cover.

decision_tree.description: Mirrors must be deployed within ~2 seconds of the counterparty finishing their sentence — any longer and the moment is gone. Listen for the trigger word; when you hear it, mirror immediately.

branches.if_you_hear: A qualifying word: 'usually', 'typically', 'for now', 'currently', 'normally', 'in most cases'.
branches.interpretation: There IS an exception. The qualifier is the door.
branches.do_not: Do not say 'so there are exceptions?' — too direct. The mirror does the same work without confrontation.

branches.if_you_hear: A specific number — price, fee, term length, limit.
branches.interpretation: Numbers spoken aloud often have flex the rep is mentally bracketing.
branches.say: Mirror the number with upward inflection: '$200?' / '12 months?'
branches.do_not: Do not add 'is that flexible?' — the mirror already asks that.

branches.if_you_hear: Internal jargon you don't know the meaning of.
branches.interpretation: Hidden product/policy layer. Probably has tiers you haven't explored.
branches.say: Mirror the jargon: 'A legacy tier?' / 'Override code?'
branches.do_not: Do not pretend to know what it means. The mirror is a free education that doesn't reveal ignorance.

branches.interpretation: The rep is uncertain. There's space to move.
branches.say: Mirror the hedge: 'You think?' / 'Might be able to?'
branches.do_not: Do not call out the uncertainty directly ('you don't sound sure'). Mirror is friendlier.

branches.if_you_hear: An emotionally charged statement ('that's just how it is', 'I'm being honest with you').
branches.interpretation: Emotional, not informational. Mirror is the WRONG tool.
branches.do_not: Do not mirror emotion — it reads as mockery.

branches.if_you_hear: A flat 'no' with no surrounding qualifying language.
branches.interpretation: Not enough material to mirror. Need a different tool.
branches.say: Use a calibrated question from `02-calibrated-questions`: 'What about it doesn't work for you?'

branches.interpretation: Rep is overexplaining; they're not sure of their position OR they're stalling.
branches.say: Mirror the SINGLE most consequential phrase from the ramble: the one with the constraint.
branches.do_not: Do not mirror the whole sentence. Pick 1-3 words.

branches.interpretation: Mirror is working — they're constructing the elaboration.
branches.do_not: Do not rescue. The mirror's power comes from forcing them to fill the air.

boundaries: Do NOT mirror more than 4 times in a single 30-minute call — diminishing returns and reads as parrot.
Do NOT mirror emotional outbursts — label them instead.
Do NOT mirror sarcastically — tone must be genuinely curious.
Do NOT mirror your own previous mirror (loops happen — break with a label or calibrated question).
success_signals: Rep elaborates on the mirrored word for 20+ seconds, revealing nuance.
Rep softens the qualifier ('well, usually — but I could see making an exception if…').
Rep explains the jargon AND volunteers an adjacent tier you didn't know existed.
Number you mirrored gets moved by the rep without you asking.
failure_signals: Rep responds 'yes, that's what I said' — your mirror was clumsy or too obvious. Pivot to label.
Rep gets annoyed — you've mirrored too often or with wrong tone.
Mirror produces only a one-word confirmation, not elaboration — try a calibrated question instead.

exit_strategy.on_success: Once the mirror surfaces the exception/flex, immediately use it: 'Got it — and how do I get access to that legacy tier?'
exit_strategy.on_failure: If mirrors aren't producing elaboration, the rep may be too disengaged or too scripted. Switch tools: try `01-tactical-empathy-labeling` to break the script.

required_user_info: None directly — this is a real-time technique. But knowing the user's goal helps pick WHICH word to mirror.
